informal: Suppose that $T \in \mathcal{L}(V)$ has $\operatorname{dim} V$ distinct eigenvalues and that $S \in \mathcal{L}(V)$ has the same eigenvectors as $T$ (not necessarily with the same eigenvalues). Prove that $S T=T S$.
lean:
theorem Axler_exercise_5_20 {F V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Field F]
  [Module F V] [FiniteDimensional F V] {S T : End F V}
  (h1 : card (T.Eigenvalues) = finrank F V)
  (h2 : ∀ v : V, (∃ c : F, v ∈ eigenspace S c) ↔ (∃ c : F, v ∈ eigenspace T c)) :
  S * T = T * S
reasoning: The Lean signature matches the informal statement. `card (T.Eigenvalues) = finrank F V` correctly expresses "T has dim V distinct eigenvalues" (since `Eigenvalues` is the subtype of scalars that are actual eigenvalues). Hypothesis h2 says v ∈ eigenspace S c for some c iff v ∈ eigenspace T c for some c; the zero vector trivially lies in every eigenspace on both sides, so this condition is equivalent (for nonzero v) to "v is an eigenvector of S iff v is an eigenvector of T", which is precisely "S has the same eigenvectors as T (not necessarily with the same eigenvalues)". The conclusion `S * T = T * S` is the commutation in `End F V`. Background assumptions (finite-dimensional F-module) match L(V) for V finite-dimensional, which is required for "dim V distinct eigenvalues" to make sense.
